topics.title: Authentication System Design — JWT, SSO & Refresh Tokens
topics.description: Authentication system design covers: stateless JWT vs stateful sessions, refresh token rotation, SSO (Single Sign-On) flows, OAuth2 authorization code flow, and token revocation strategies. The core trade-off is security vs scalability. Sessions require centralized storage (Redis) but enable instant revocation. JWT is stateless and scalable but revocation requires a blocklist or short expiry. SSO delegates authentication to a central Identity Provider (IdP) — Google, Okta, Keycloak.
topics.story: Your first version: username/password stored in DB, sessions in memory. Works for 1 server. You scale to 10 servers — server 2 doesn't know about the session server 1 created. Fix: centralize sessions in Redis. New problem: Redis becomes a single point of failure. JWT fix: embed identity in the token — every server can verify it independently. New problem: you can't revoke JWT until it expires. Fix: short expiry (15min) + refresh tokens (7 days) + refresh token revocation in DB. Each 'fix' introduces a new trade-off — system design is managing these trade-offs.
topics.analogy: Session = hotel key card tied to reception's record (centralized, revocable). JWT = a self-contained ID card with name and expiry printed on it (decentralized, can't be cancelled mid-period). Refresh token = the master key in the hotel safe (long-lived, only used to get new room cards). SSO = using your corporate badge to enter multiple partner buildings (one identity, many services). OAuth2 = letting the parking valet (third-party app) use your car without giving them your house key.
topics.memoryAnchor: JWT: access token (15min) + refresh token (7 days httpOnly cookie). Refresh flow: access expired → POST /auth/refresh with cookie → new access token. Revocation: blocklist in Redis or refresh token rotation (rotate on use, invalidate old). SSO: Identity Provider (IdP) → Service Provider (SP). OAuth2 flows: authorization_code (web), client_credentials (M2M), implicit (deprecated). PKCE: code_challenge prevents auth code interception. Token storage: access in memory (XSS safe), refresh in httpOnly cookie (CSRF safe).
topics.firstPrinciples: Authentication = prove who you are. Authorization = what you can do. JWT is a signed claim — the server trusts the signature, not a DB lookup. Stateless means no shared state between server instances — enables horizontal scaling. The signature uses HMAC-SHA256 (symmetric, one secret shared) or RS256 (asymmetric, private key signs, public key verifies — better for microservices).
topics.bruteForce: HTTP Basic Auth: base64(username:password) in every request header. DB lookup on every request. No token, no session — just re-authenticate each request. Secure only over HTTPS. Works but DB hit per request is expensive. No 'remember me', no token refresh.
topics.optimizedApproach: Login → server returns short-lived JWT (15min) + sets httpOnly cookie with refresh token (7 days). Client stores JWT in memory (not localStorage — XSS risk). Every request: Authorization: Bearer {accessToken}. On 401: POST /auth/refresh (cookie sent automatically) → new JWT. On logout: delete refresh token from DB, clear cookie. Revocation: check refresh token revocation list on /auth/refresh only — not on every API call.
topics.whenToUse: JWT: stateless APIs, microservices, mobile apps. Sessions + Redis: when you need instant revocation (financial apps, high-security). SSO/OAuth2: multiple services sharing identity, enterprise auth, third-party login. API keys: M2M, public API rate-limiting. mTLS: zero-trust internal service auth.

examples.title: Refresh Token Rotation Flow
examples.explanation: Refresh token rotation: each use of a refresh token issues a NEW refresh token and marks the old one as used. If the old token is used again (potential theft), ALL sessions for that user are revoked. Access tokens are short-lived (15min) — even if stolen, the window of exploit is small. Refresh tokens are long-lived but stored in httpOnly cookies (not accessible to JavaScript).
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: POST /api/auth/login returns JWT (15min) in response body + refresh token in httpOnly cookie. React stores JWT in memory/state. On 401: automatically POST /api/auth/refresh → new JWT. On logout: DELETE /api/auth/logout clears cookie + DB entry. Google OAuth2 uses similar refresh token rotation.

examples.title: OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow (SSO Design)
examples.explanation: The authorization code flow keeps tokens server-side — the authorization code is short-lived and single-use. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) prevents code interception attacks by binding the code to a client-generated secret. The state parameter prevents CSRF. After getting Google's tokens, you issue YOUR OWN JWT — don't pass Google's access token to your API.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: Spring Security OAuth2 handles steps 1-4. OAuth2SuccessHandler creates/finds user by email, issues JWT, redirects to frontend with token. Frontend: /oauth2/authorize/google → Google login → callback with your JWT. For enterprise: Okta/Keycloak replaces Google as IdP — same flow, different provider URL.

problems.title: Token Bucket Rate Limiter
problems.pattern: Token bucket, rate limiting, time-based refill
problems.hint1: Track: {tokens: double, lastRefill: long} per user.
problems.hint2: On each call: elapsed = now - lastRefill; tokens = min(capacity, tokens + elapsed * refillRate).
problems.hint3: If tokens >= 1: tokens--; return true. Else return false.

problems.title: Session vs JWT Trade-off Analysis
problems.hint1: Session: ConcurrentHashMap.get(sessionId) — O(1) but requires shared storage.
problems.hint2: JWT: recompute HMAC with SecretKeySpec + Mac.doFinal — O(n) but no shared state.
problems.hint3: JWT is slower per request but scales without centralized session store.
problems.sampleOutput: New access_token + new refresh_token issued; old refresh_token invalidated

problems.title: Design: Describe SSO Flow in 5 Steps
problems.description:
Implement OAuth2 Authorization Code flow from scratch.

**Flow:**
```
1. User clicks 'Login with Google'
2. Redirect to: accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth?client_id=...&redirect_uri=...&scope=email
3. User consents
4. Google redirects to: /oauth2/callback?code=AUTHORIZATION_CODE
5. Server exchanges code for tokens: POST accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/token
6. Server gets user info: GET www.googleapis.com/userinfo/v2/me
7. Create/find user; issue app JWT
```

**Security requirements:**
- `state` parameter to prevent CSRF
- PKCE for public clients
- Code is single-use, short-lived (10 minutes)

**Key insight:** Authorization code flow keeps tokens server-side. User never sees the access_token. `state` parameter must be validated to prevent CSRF attacks on the callback.
problems.hint1: Central IdP (Okta/Keycloak) stores user credentials and issues tokens.
problems.hint2: Each app (Service Provider) redirects to IdP for auth, receives token, verifies signature.
problems.hint3: Logout: central logout at IdP + back-channel logout to all SPs.
problems.testCases: Describes 5-step SSO flow with redirect, token exchange, auto-login, and logout
problems.sampleInput: User completes Google OAuth2 consent; code=abc123 arrives at /oauth2/callback
problems.sampleOutput: Code exchanged for Google access_token; user email fetched; app JWT issued
